original_abstract:
Efficient electrochemical conversion of CO2 to methane (CH4) offers a viable pathway for carbon-neutral fuel production, yet achieving high selectivity at industrial current densities remains a significant challenge. This study presents a multi-scale catalyst design strategy that integrates the engineering of atomic-scale electronic properties with the optimization of the mesoscale reaction microenvironment. We developed an interconnected mesoporous carbon fiber (IPCF) support derived from block copolymers to host atomically dispersed Cu sites. This unique architecture provides H2O-deficient, CO2-rich, and long-range confined mesochannels that effectively suppress the hydrogen evolution reaction while increasing the residence time of CO intermediates to promote deep reduction.

Crucially, operando X-ray absorption and Raman spectroscopy provide evidence of the dynamic in-situ reconstruction of isolated Cu–N3 moieties into N,OH-immobilized Cu3 clusters under reaction conditions. Theoretical calculations reveal that these Cu3 clusters possess an optimal *CO adsorption affinity and a lower energy barrier for *CO hydrogenation relative to C−C coupling, fundamentally steering the selectivity toward methane. Benefiting from the synergy between these reconstructed active sites and the bicontinuous mesoporous framework, the catalyst achieves a remarkable CH4 Faradaic efficiency of 74.2% at a current density of 300 mA cm−2. These findings establish a robust design principle for navigating complex reaction pathways in interfacial electrochemical systems.
humanized_abstract:
Efficient conversion of CO2 to CH4 through electrochemical reaction is considered to be a feasible method for producing clean energy. However, the low selectivity is still a major obstacle for this method to be widely applied at an industrial current density. In this work, we proposed a multi-scale catalyst design strategy that links the regulation of the electronic properties at the atomic scale and the mesoscale reaction environment. By combining atomically dispersed Cu species on an interconnected mesoporous carbon fiber (IPCF) support derived from block copolymers, a H2O-deficient, CO2-rich, and long-range confined mesochannels structure was achieved, leading to the suppression of the hydrogen evolution reaction and an increased residence time for CO species, thus resulting in a high selectivity for methane.

Operando XAS and Raman spectroscopy performed over the electrolysis reveal that the isolated Cu–N3 species evolve in situ into N,OH-immobilized Cu3 clusters. Modeling studies demonstrate that the Cu3 clusters possess the optimal *CO adsorption strength and barrier height for the *CO hydrogenation to ethylene over that for C–C coupling reaction, hence resulting in selective production of methane. In-situ synergy between the regenerated catalyst structure and the bicontinuous mesoporous morphology leads to remarkable CH4 Faradaic efficiency as high as 74.2% at a current density of 300 mA cm−2, that shed light on feasible strategies to steer complex electrochemical reactions.
